data.title: Apple: Beware The Lollapalooza Effect
data.description:
Really big effects, lollapalooza effects, will often come only from large combinations of factors. - Charlie Munger

It is difficult to think of anyone who has ever had a better understanding about how society and the economy work than Charlie Munger. He took the time to understand the financials, the incentives, and the psychology behind how society operates. One particularly interesting mental model that he used was the "lollapalooza effect", which he used to describe situations where a confluence of factors came together to deliver an incredibly powerful impact. This impact could be positive, or negative, and the magnitude often takes most observers by surprise. It could be said that Berkshire Hathaway's impressive returns are the result of the "lollapalooza effect" of the combined skills and wisdom of Warren Buffett, Charlie Munger, the strong U.S. economic expansion during the time they were investing, and the added leverage from their insurance operations, among other factors.

It appears that Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) (TSX:AAPL:CA) might experience a "lollapalooza effect", but of the negative kind, as a result of a number of negative factors that are likely to impact the company in the coming years. First, most of the products that the company sells are discretionary, with consumers having the option to postpone purchases if their economic situation deteriorates. They are also extremely reliant on overseas manufacturing, with parts and assembly mostly done in Asian countries. If tariffs remain in place, Apple is likely to experience higher production costs that the company will either have to absorb and see profit margins deteriorate, or pass the price increase to customers, which would likely reduce sales considerably. In foreign markets, Apple could also be impacted by retaliatory tariffs, for example Germany and France are considering targeting digital services, or even consumer backlash could lead customers to buy other brands as is happening in China.

All of these is happening at a time when Apple was already struggling to deliver meaningful revenue growth, and where consumer weakness was becoming evident. The added stress from tariffs is likely to send the U.S. economy into recession, as we recently discussed in another article, and this time it will be extremely difficult for the Fed to come to the rescue, as both of its mandates will be under pressure. With the ballooning government deficit and the inflationary impact from tariffs putting pressure on the Fed to increase rates, while higher unemployment and declining economic growth needing the help of lower interest rates. Recession risk has significantly increased, with the likes of Goldman Sachs' (GS) and JPMorgan's (JPM) racing to increase the probabilities of a recession in the next twelve months. Selling more iPhones is going to get tricky at a time when some consumers are even resorting to buy now, pay later financing for their DoorDash (DASH) orders.

Financials

For almost three years now, Apple's revenue and earnings have basically stalled. In fact, revenue growth has barely been above the inflation rate in recent quarters. The one bright spot has been the company's operating margin, which has seen a meaningful increase, but this has failed to translate to large earnings per share (EPS) increases, partly as a result of increased stock-based compensation.

Innovation Decline

Even more concerning, it appears Apple is no longer the innovation powerhouse it used to be under Steve Jobs. The company has failed to deliver on some of its most promising efforts, including the "Project Titan" Apple car. The company spent over a decade and billions of dollars, with little to show for it in the end. At the same time, companies with significantly less resources like Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACY) basically accomplished Apple's vision with a fraction of the resources and in record time. Xiaomi is expected to sell over 350,000 of its Smart EVs in 2025, and that could have been Apple.

These cars are not only electric and connected to the Xiaomi ecosystem, but are rapidly having autonomous driving features added. These include automated parking capabilities and traffic light real-time alerts. Meanwhile, Apple is failing to even get AI to interpret news and messages correctly. A good example is one case where a user reported that a text from his mother reading “That hike almost killed me!” had been turned into “Attempted suicide, but recovered.” To be fair, trying to summarize group chats is not an easy task, which might justify the summary "Rude comments exchanged". This has prompted articles saying that Apple's AI is anything but intelligent, and the company admitting errors and shaking up its AI executive ranks.

Stock-based Compensation

Despite spending ever-increasing amounts on stock-based compensation, Apple has failed to retain some key talent. This includes the departure of legendary Apple designer Jony Ive in 2019, and more generally, the company sliding in the "Best Place to Work" rankings. Apparently, part of the reason is related to the return-to-work policy, but it probably does not help when the most ambitious engineering projects like the electric car project see multiple leadership changes and end up canceled.

One thing to notice about Apple's stock-based compensation is that it has not only increased in absolute terms, which could be justified by its increased market capitalization, but also as a percentage of its quarterly revenue.

Future Outlook

In the near term, Apple might see a boost to sales, with customers panic buying iPhones and other Apple products before prices increase as a result of tariffs. Some analysts are even speculating we could see $2,300 iPhones in a worst-case scenario. This would no doubt reduce demand and have a significant negative impact on Apple's revenue and profitability. The company also has a lot of work to do to get its VR and AI strategy right.

These developments are starting to be reflected in analyst earnings revisions, with 26 downward revisions in the last 3 months, compared to only 10 upward revisions according to tracking by Seeking Alpha. If current tariffs remain in place, we believe earnings estimates will have to be revised down more meaningfully, especially should other countries retaliate with their own tariffs on U.S. products and services.

Valuation

Despite the considerable revenue and profit growth deceleration, Apple's valuation is meaningfully above its ten-year average multiples for Price/Sales and Price/Earnings. Adding the increased recession and tariff risks, we find it extremely difficult to justify the current valuation, which is meaningfully above the company's historical average. This can also be seen in the extremely low current dividend yield, roughly half the 10-year average.

Risks

While we believe Apple is likely to remain profitable even during the current environment, and we do not think it is a good short idea, we do believe the high valuation and stalling growth means a high likelihood that the company will underperform the market going forward. The biggest risk we see for Apple is the lollapalooza effect of a number of negative factors. These include stalling revenue and profit growth, impacts from higher costs from U.S. tariffs and potential retaliation from other countries, signs that innovation from the company is declining, and rapidly increasing risks of a U.S. recession.

Conclusion

It is clear that multiple headwinds are adding up for Apple, and this is being reflected in declining earnings estimates. While it is difficult to precisely forecast the impact that tariffs, the potential retaliation, and a U.S. recession would have on the company, it is important to remember that most of its products remain discretionary purchases. Even before the current developments, Apple was already delivering disappointing revenue and profit growth, very poor results from its innovation initiatives, and an elevated valuation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TLT: An Unique Asymmetrical Opportunity
data.description:
The stock market is fragile, there's no doubt about that. However, few market commentators have highlighted the contemporaneous support for the bond market. While the S&P 500 slid by over 10% in the two trading sessions following President Trump's reciprocal tariff announcement, treasury bonds gained traction, suggesting the U.S. isn't totally uninvestable. Instead, asset class rotation seems like a viable solution.

In our view, long-term bonds are a steady option, which is why we assigned a bullish rating to the PIMCO 25+ Year Zero Coupon U.S. Treasury Index Exchange-Traded Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:ZROZ) a while back. This analysis covers a similar vehicle with slightly different key rate duration, namely the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT).

Without further delay, here's why we think TLT could present asymmetrical results for the foreseeable future.

Fund Overview

The iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF or TLT for short is a bucketed bond ETF, providing investors with thematic exposure to 20+ year treasury bonds. Note however that the vehicle's effective duration is 16.13, meaning it's more likely to react to 16 year key rates than 20+ year rates for now. The fund possesses a reasonable amount of convexity (3.53), enhancing upside possibilities while cushioning downside. In addition, TLT ETF has a negative option-adjusted spread, suggesting it's a hedge against credit risk.

Historical data shows that TLT ETF's income component is salient, likely due to its reliance on coupon income and not because of capital gain distributions. Nevertheless, the volatility embedded in the current market environment warrants close attention to capital gains/loss dynamics.

Market Commentary

A Systematic Argument Screams 'Buy'

As explained in two of last week's articles, we think a U.S. recession scenario is gathering pace. Some argue that tariffs will accelerate inflation, leading to higher bonds yields. However, we've assumed the opposite stance, arguing that the global trade war will diminish consumer sentiment, concurrently enhancing a recession scenario, especially as the U.S. economy is arguably in its late cycle. As an external reference, Goldman Sachs (GS) agrees with our outlook, as the investment bank recently assigned a 45% probability to its recession scenario analysis.

The U.S. yield curve has flattened in the past month. Considering the above-mentioned, we believe a reduction in level and possible inversion is inevitable. With that being said, 16-20 year key rates remain relatively pent-up, allowing investors to lock-in yields before lower yields materialise. For those unaware, yield reduction would likely lead to capital gains without sacrificing much coupon income (unless yields stay lower-for-longer). Thus, we see this as an incredible active opportunity for excess return-seeking investors.

Many might argue that a recession will lead to steepening of the curve. We agree with the notion. Though, our argument here is that the bond market will price a hard landing recession in advance, providing investors an active opportunity now instead of during a recession. In fact, we think it would be wise to ride intermediate-to-longer term yields into a recession and pivot into other opportunities once a recession has materialised. Alternatively, we'd start sliding into riskier assets in a deep recession scenario on the basis of "buy-the-dip."

However... Technical Aspects Might Restrict Systematic Support

Although we have reasonable confidence in our fundamental argument, technical aspects have to be considered before drawing a holistic conclusion. As illustrated below, the 20-year bid-to-cover ratio remains within range, suggesting ex-Fed open market supply/demand remains undeterred. TLT has traded flat year-over-year, therefore a change in supply/demand dynamics will likely need to occur for it to shift by an asymmetrical amount.

The SOMA, which communicates the New York Fed's open market operations, suggests lower treasury demand occurred in 2024. We anticipate the trajectory to change in 2025, yet, our opinion is merely an outlook and not necessarily the ground truth. Thus, a possibility remains that the supply/demand outlook at the Fed-level could remain docile for the time being.

Lastly, for this section. A glance at the Treasury Surplus echoes worries. Although the U.S. Fed would typically start buying bonds in a lowering-rate scenario, the deficit poses obstacles, suggesting the FED could finance rather than invest for the foreseeable future. Such a scenario could pose telling headwinds for TLT ETF from a supply/demand vantage point.

TLT's Key Metrics

I re-inserted the portfolio characteristics table for convenience. The data shows that TLT ETF has a 30-day SEC yield of 4.51%, about 4 basis points higher than ZROZ ETF. We find this comparison commendable; TLT has less duration exposure than ZROZ, yet its SEC yield remains higher. Moreover, TLT ETF has a yield-to-worst of approximately 4.53%, a compelling figure, suggesting the ETF has locked-in higher-than-normal yields for the time being.

In our view, an additional benefit of TLT ETF is its monthly distribution, allowing investors to lower their liquidity risk. In addition, the ETF has an expense ratio of 0.15%, which, according to our calculations, if compounded for ten years adds up to around 1.51%, a rather docile amount.

Conclusion

Considering the possibility of a hard landing, we think TLT ETF presents a unique opportunity to benefit from a sharp drop in the U.S. yield curve. In addition, the ETF has locked-in lucrative yields during a higher-for-longer interest rate environment. While we acknowledge risks such as supply/demand uncertainties and possible yield steepening during a recession, we see TLT ETF as an excellent active opportunity, especially if combined with ZROZ.

data.title: That Was A Dead Cat Bounce, Not A Turnaround
data.description:
As the reciprocal tariffs are about to take effect soon, market hopes for a swift reversal of tariffs are evaporating. On the one hand, Trump's administration is repeatedly emphasizing that the US is engaged in trade negotiations with dozens of countries. On the other hand, Trump has slapped China with a 104% tariff rate without a clear perspective for trade negotiations. Thus, there are two key uncertainties regarding the tariffs: how fast the Trump administration will negotiate on trade with the rest of the world, and for how long the trade war with China may last given that both sides seem to be driven by pride and not by readiness to talk.

104% Tariff Rate On Chinese Goods Is Coming

A week after the Liberation Day, the first outlines of the Trump administration's strategy are beginning to emerge. From what I see now, Trump is ready to negotiate with everyone except China, which immediately retaliated to the US' reciprocal tariffs.

My biggest concern so far is that the trade confrontation between the US and China is evolving into a "who blinks first" game, where both sides have the incentive to cause an exhaustion of the opposing side before engaging in negotiations.

In this regard, here's my vision of the next moves of the Trump administration:

  1. Negotiating trade with some of the important trade partners (Canada, Mexico, Japan, India), striking relatively easy deals;
  2. Negotiating trade with the European Union (which may be tougher to do, given it's a bloc of countries and not a one country);
  3. Negotiating trade with China after trade deals with other countries are settled.

While it's too early to speculate on negotiation timelines, my gut feeling tells me that these negotiations can't be done fast enough to avoid any noticeable damage to the global economy and the US economy specifically.

A Dead Cat Bounce

The sharp rally we saw in the first half of the trading session on Tuesday, with the S&P 500 index gaining almost 4%, is consistent with the historical behavior. The Sentiment Indicator from Goldman Sachs indicates that the extremely low level of investor sentiment (below -2.0) has typically been followed by strong positive returns, averaging roughly 2.6% over the subsequent two weeks.

Nonetheless, what seemed to be a market rally after a harsh sell-off turned out to be nothing more but a temporary relief before another day in the negative zone. After the sharp bounce, the S&P 500 lost all of its growth during the day and closed at -1.57%, going down by more than 5% from the day's high.

For me, it looked like the markets felt enthusiastic about the prospect of quick deals with the US' key trading partners, but the confirmation of the 104% tariff rate for China amid a lack of any positive headlines was a cold shower for the markets. In the terminology of bear markets, this is a perfect example of a dead cat bounce, a short-lived recovery of asset prices before another dip.

Why I'm becoming increasingly more skeptical about the swift resolution of this trade war? Let's look at Canada and Mexico. Both faced the US tariffs more than a month ago, and both are the key trade partners for the United States. So far, I haven't seen a single news headline recently about any developments in trade negotiations between the US and Mexico or Canada. Given that we haven't seen any deals with just two countries, can we expect rapid negotiations between the US and the rest of the world? I don't think so.

In a recent interview with Fox News, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent commented on the probable length of trade negotiations:

"So it’s going to be a busy April, May, maybe into June".

Thus, in my opinion, investors should brace for at least one or two months of trade negotiations, and it remains a question if Trump announces any kind of delay or pause of the reciprocal tariffs for some countries.

At the same time, in the longer term, history suggests a more positive picture for the S&P 500. When market fear, measured by the VIX, reaches extreme levels, history suggests it's a clear buy signal for the long term.

If we look at the highest weekly VIX closes since 1990, the subsequent average 1-year S&P 500 total return for 21 highest VIX readings was 39%. While past performance never guarantees future results, the consistency of this pattern provides some evidence that peak VIX numbers have historically marked attractive long-term buying opportunities. However, investors should keep in mind that the 1-year S&P 500 average forward return for all other periods between 1990 and 2025 is significantly lower, amounting to just 12%.

It May Get Much Worse Before It Gets Any Better

According to Goldman Sachs, S&P 500 EPS growth estimates haven't caught up yet with the new tariff reality. While the Q1 2025 EPS growth is estimated at 6% year-over-year, the Q2 EPS estimate remains at 9%, not to mention 11% YoY EPS growth for the second half of 2025. These "pre-tariff" estimates look like a vulnerable target for a downward revision if we see a prolonged trade war.

Companies may come first, downgrading their guidance amid the tariffs. After that, analyst estimates may follow, creating a wave of rating downgrades that may trigger sales on the side of institutional investors and passively managed investment vehicles like ETFs.

The Bottom Line: It's Too Early To Call The Bottom

I may be very well wrong, and the Trump administration manages to negotiate with everyone in a matter of weeks, providing much-needed relief for markets and the economy. Nonetheless, what we've seen so far doesn't provide reasons for optimism, at least in the short term.

In this tricky environment, investors should be extremely cautious when it comes to buying the dip. In my view, it's better to buy later when we see any degree of economic certainty instead of trying to catch the bottom of the market. Thus, investors shouldn't be in a hurry with buying, and cash-like tools (in the form of the $SGOV ETF, for example) remain one of the few ways to hedge a portfolio from a downside risk in this market.

data.title: The Tale Of Tariffs Round Two For The U.S. Economy
data.description:
By Eric Winograd

How might the recently announced US trade measures translate into economic reality?

With the latest round of US tariff measures announced in early April, we’ve downgraded our US economic outlook. We expect slower growth and higher inflation for 2025. If this plays out, we think the Fed will cut policy rates by 75 basis points in 2025, possibly more.

The back and forth over specific tariff measures remains very fluid, as recent headlines can attest, and if the policies announced April 2 don’t stay in place, the situation could change considerably.

But if they do stay in effect, the impact could be dramatic. We think those policies, combined with cuts in domestic spending and government jobs, would reduce growth in 2025 US GDP to a range of 0% to 0.5%, with a significant chance of recession.

Tariff Impact Flows Through to US Price Levels

The April tariffs have boosted the effective tariff rate by roughly 25% from last year, which we expect will push prices for US consumers and businesses higher. Slower growth and declining commodity prices may blunt some of the impact, but we’ve raised our inflation forecast for this year to 4% for the core Consumer Price Index. That’s about 1.5% higher than core inflation would have been without tariffs.

Those higher prices equate to a roughly $4,000 cost for the average US family, which is almost 10% of post-tax median income. It’s true that price increases in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic were even bigger, but aid from the federal government helped US households to weather that earlier period. Such payments seem highly unlikely this time around.

The expected slowdown isn’t a directional change from our prior forecasts. Household spending has already slowed this year (Display) and falling measures of consumer confidence suggest more to come - even before last week’s tariff announcements. Of course, confidence can be volatile, and other factors can influence spending. But we think the impending tariffs on top of existing economic data skew risks clearly to the downside going forward.

Strong Starting Point Likely Means Economic Slowdown, Not Collapse

At this point, we expect a slowdown, but it’s important to remember that the starting point for the US economy is strong. The labor market is robust and stable, providing households with a steady source of income. Households don’t have the excess savings cushion that they had post-pandemic, but labor income has outpaced inflation this cycle - if that holds, the US economy won’t collapse.

The March payrolls report also illustrates the job market’s resilience. The headline gain of 228,000 jobs keeps the longer-term average hiring rate steady at around 150,000 per month.

With migration flows having dried up last year and remaining subdued this year, that’s more than enough to absorb new entrants to the labor force and keep the unemployment rate steady at around 4%. That’s up from the post-pandemic overheating but still very low historically.

The administration’s efforts to reduce government employment appear to be bearing fruit; federal employment has fallen for three consecutive months. Beyond that, the basic patterns of the labor market remain in place, with healthcare the strongest sector. The combination of solid hiring and decent wage growth is keeping the growth rate of the household paycheck in positive territory after accounting for inflation (Display).

Multiple 2025 Rate Cuts Likely on Tap…if Inflation Behaves

For now, we expect the Fed to wait for data to guide its decisions. If the economy slows, as we expect it will, the Fed will be inclined to cut rates even if price levels are high. The view is that actual inflation tells us what the economy was doing but not what it will do. The Fed has cut rates before with inflation elevated, and we expect it to do so again unless - a very big “unless” - inflation expectations become unanchored.

A rise in inflation expectations - not the actual inflation rate itself - was the defining feature of the 1970s inflation shock, making the initial shock persist. The Fed has learned from that experience, and Chair Jay Powell’s recent comments make it clear that inflation expectations are the key variable to monitor right now. The vast majority of indicators, including market-based (Display), still point to inflation expectations staying anchored. The University of Michigan survey is the notable exception.

If expectations remain well anchored, the Fed will be able to cut rates and support the economy. We expect 75 basis points in rate cuts over the rest of 2025 starting in the summer. We see a greater risk that rate cuts top that level than fall short of it. The policy rate at the end of this cycle will likely be below 3%, a level intended to stimulate economic growth.

Markets will continue to wrestle with the implications of an expanding trade war. As headlines suggest, other countries may not give in but retaliate, escalating the conflict, and there’s ample room for the US to do the same.

While it’s possible that some of the announced tariffs won’t “stick,” we think most of them likely will, and we haven’t yet seen the impact flow through to the economic data. That means this story is far from over.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. Views are subject to revision over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: How Data Is Reshaping Agricultural Markets
data.description:
By Debbie Carlson

At a Glance

  • As technology and data collection practices continue to evolve, agricultural commodities are becoming more specialized
  • Rising consumer demand for organic and identity-preserved crops is reemphasizing the importance of traceability

When Matt Bennett, a seventh-generation grain farmer based in Shelby County, Ill., plants his crops in the spring, he can download data from his tractor to precisely track the types of hybrids he’s sown in his fields, where they’re located and how far apart they are spaced, tailored to the soil type.

As the crops grow, he uses weather apps to measure rainfall without having to drive to the fields and measure moisture. He layers thermal imaging maps with yield maps to fine-tune what production might look like in the growing season. After harvest, he will update his yield maps and overlay those with fertilizer maps. Working with his seed dealer, they can decide for the following year what to plant, where to plant it and the best type of seed to use.

“These tools have helped us to maximize efficiency,” Bennett says.

Not only is that data useful for Bennett to grow crops now, in the future he might be able to use that data as another valuable commodity. As technology advances and the ability to collect and store data grows, commodity production has become more granular.

Technology has allowed farmers, traders and end users to get market intelligence in a digital fashion to make smarter, more efficient decisions tailored to the supply and demand of the market. The industry can use data to verify where crops were grown and if they’ve adhered to certain growing practices with specific attributes, which may allow them to command a premium.

Following the Trends

Rising demand for organic and identity-preserved crops inspired Kellee James, founder and CEO of Mercaris, now Argus AgriMarkets Organic, to create a market information service for these niche crops. Prices for crops such as organic corn or non-genetically modified soybeans don’t always follow the larger pricing trends in the commodities markets.

The divergent pricing trend represented risk for the industry. “We saw a gap in the marketplace,” she says, and thought at the time, “let's get in there and help people along the whole supply chain to understand those markets, supply, demand and prices."

Working with producers, merchandisers and others in the physical commodity industry, a dozen years ago she created an online dashboard of news, insight and data, plus prices and trends dedicated to those in the identity-preserved sector to help them make more informed decisions.

Jeff Schreiner, SVP of Global Collaboration at Cultura Technologies, a growing global collaborative of Agri-Food focused technology businesses, says the ability to collect more data and network that information together means data can enhance the value of a commodity and may become a product itself.

For example, he says, traceability has always been important to agriculture, and with new technologies that allow geolocating of products, this enables end users to follow not only where a commodity came from, but how it was treated along the way. Rather than being reactive, this allows producers to be proactive to prevent issues downstream that can be valuable to consumers, especially in the organic or sustainable industries.

“If our industry comes together to link production and the values that the consumer really wants with the value chain – and be able to prove it – that's a completely new, proactive marketplace that doesn't exist today,” he says.

Another example is the rising interest in how to value carbon. Farmers who raise crops for biofuels, and can measure the carbon intensity of how they treat and grow the crops, can not only sell their crops to an ethanol plant, but potentially receive additional financial value for their methods, Schreiner says.

Challenges in Tech Adoption

Technology is also bringing transparency to Brazil’s commodity sector. Grao Direto is an online marketplace for pricing physical grains that brings buyers and sellers together. Fred Marques, Chief Technology Officer at Grao Direto, says the platform gives farmers and grain companies greater market intelligence by posting prices from 13,000 locations across the country, and does away with the phone-based negotiations between a single buyer and seller, which was common in Brazil.

Still, technology can intimidate. To counteract that, Marques says they talk to those grain brokers and explain how digitalizing the process will allow them to work with more farmers.

“If you have 400 farmers, you can’t talk to every one of them every day,” he says. The platform also aims to build trust between buyer and seller by verifying information, such as ensuring sustainability compliance by the grower for buyers who need assurances and offering financing for farmers, he says.

Bennett says one of the immediate challenges with technology is that new software can be expensive and with farm incomes pressured by high input costs and low farmgate prices, it may be difficult for farmers to invest. Mixed equipment brands and their technology can also be a challenge, since it may take producers extra steps to have the equipment communicate, such as hitching one company’s planter equipment to another’s tractor.

An Ongoing Evolution

There are also concerns about balancing privacy and transparency. James says in the beginning it was difficult for her to convince participants in the identity-preserved industry to share commercially sensitive information, and she says it’s still an issue for some people.

“You've got to work really hard to get people's trust and be consistent,” she says.

Schreiner concurs. "Just because you're sharing and becoming visible doesn't mean that you're losing your independence. It doesn't mean that you're creating a security or privacy or an oversight problem or regulation problem,” he says.

The agriculture industry is still in its early stages of how to handle large-scale data collection, so it is still working out questions about infrastructure, data management and the new risks these changes could introduce.

Yet, Schreiner says being one of the later industries to transform could work in agriculture’s favor, since it can look to sectors such as healthcare and finance to see how they’ve addressed those challenges.

“We're going to leverage all that research for years and years to short circuit the amount of time it takes us to figure out the mechanics of doing that,” he says.

James says with more data could come greater price-risk tools, which may eventually include insurance products tailored to these producers and end-users, or even better derivatives contracts to hedge. “If you have really good information, you can either use the tools available more efficiently, or you can come up with new tools entirely,” she says.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The Current Reality Looks Too Much Like 1929 For Comfort
data.description:
This year, investors are being treated to a major drawdown in the U.S. markets. The S&P 500 has been in correction territory for over a week, and actually entered bear market territory on Monday and Tuesday.

In this environment, many investors are wondering how much exposure to equities is appropriate given the elevated risk. An indication of sentiment is the extremely warm response to Warren Buffett’s decision to move into treasuries, which resulted in Buffett being the only person in the top 10 richest Americans to see his net worth increase this year. Treasuries themselves have outperformed stocks year to date, with the 10-year yield down 7.95% since the start of the year (a lower yield implies a higher price).

Investors’ flight to treasuries at the start of this year was likely a wise move. The speculation in the U.S. markets at the start of the year was similar to that seen in the 1920s, immediately before the Great Crash of 1929. Similarities between the two periods included a high ratio of market capitalization to gross value added; an increase in retail investors trading on margin; and historically high portfolio allocations to equities. The conditions were not exactly identical to those seen in 1929; the amount of allowable margin was lower, for example. However, investors can get around margin restrictions by using non-margin loans (e.g., lines of credit) to buy stocks. So it’s possible that the amount of leverage in the markets in late 2024 was higher than commonly thought.

Now, another similarity between the current period and the Great Depression is beginning to emerge:

A massive increase in tariffs.

Last Wednesday, Donald Trump announced a package of “reciprocal” tariffs that increased the tariff rate on many countries and regions, most notably China and the European Union (EU). The tariffs were intended to go into effect at Midnight on Wednesday. As of this writing, no deals to cut tariffs had been reached with China or the EU, although a key Trump economic advisor said discussions with “50 countries” were moving along.

This increase in tariffs is similar to that enacted by Herbert Hoover in 1930, to stem the effects of the Great Depression. The tariffs, enacted via the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, increased the tariff rate by 20% and led to a steep decline in U.S. exports due to retaliatory tariffs by other countries. The modern consensus is that the tariffs worsened the Great Depression.

Trump’s retaliatory tariffs resemble those enacted in the Smoot-Hawley tariff act in two ways:

  1. The rates are generally high. The average tariff rate after the implementation of the Hawley Smoot tariff act was 59%. The average with Trump’s reciprocal tariffs isn’t quite there yet, but some countries, such as China, Vietnam and Bangladesh, face similar rates.

  2. Tariffs increased massively in a relatively short span of time. Prior to Wednesday’s announcement, the U.S. generally had low tariffs on most countries. By Tuesday, it had a rate of 104% on China, and near 50% on several other Asian countries.

So, we had market dynamics similar to those seen in 1929 at the beginning of the year, and now have a tariff policy similar to that enacted in 1930 which was seen as worsening the Great Depression. The similarities between this situation and the early Great Depression period are hard to ignore. Of course, there are differences, too: for one thing, prices generally fell during the Great Depression. Today, with the U.S. having much of its manufacturing abroad, it’s hard to picture that happening (unless there is substantial tariff relief), so perhaps a stagflation type of scenario will play out here. Nobody knows what the future holds, but a worst-case scenario with the tariffs would be extremely negative, and stock market drawdowns in similar historical scenarios have been as severe as 89%.

The Investing Takeaway

The big investing takeaway from all of this is that a higher allocation to lower risk assets is ideal. In the Great Depression, U.S. treasuries provided some of the safest returns amid stock market pandemonium, rising in value and providing a little interest income while stocks were tanking. Investors who bet heavily on treasuries risk having others mock them as ‘boring,’ but the fact is that they tend to be the best assets to own during recessions. They pay a little interest and also experience capital gains when interest rates come down–the Fed tends to cut rates during recessions, so there is some hope of that happening (though the tariff-driven price hikes point in the opposite direction).

There are also treasury-like assets that serve as proxies for treasuries. Examples include certificates of deposit (CDs) and money market funds. CDs are insured by the U.S. government up to about $250,000, making them desirable for retail investors not handling millions of dollars.

I’m not saying that investors should go 100% into treasuries and treasury-like assets because of the elevated risks in the market today. There are some possible outcomes that reduce the level of uncertainty in the market: for example, a high-ranking judge striking down Trump’s ability to unilaterally enact tariffs. It that occurred and if the Supreme Court upheld the decision, then one of the major sources of risk and uncertainty in this market would be removed.

On the whole, though, I think investors would do well with a higher than normal allocation to cash in this market. I’m personally at about 50% cash (mainly term deposits) right now, and I sometimes wonder if the percentage shouldn’t be higher. The risks really are that high. Of course, there will be fantastic dip buying opportunities at some point in the future. But I’m not sure we’re there yet.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank of Korea Likely To Cut Rates In April Despite The Won's Weakness
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Private sector hiring remained weak in March

South Korea’s jobless rate rose to 2.9% in March (vs 2.7% in February, 2.8% market consensus). Clearly, the boost from the government job programmes faded, while private hiring remains sluggish. The health and social work sectors lost 52k jobs in March, partially reversing the 334k of combined jobs both added in the first two months of the year.

Meanwhile, manufacturing and construction employment was particularly weak, down 16k and 26k, respectively. A silver lining of today’s report was wholesale and retail sales, which rose for a third straight month. Though domestic political uncertainty was removed in early April, the negative impacts from tariffs and wildfires in the southern provinces are likely to weigh on labour markets going forward. We don’t expect a sudden deterioration ahead. Rather, we see a gradual rise of the jobless rate in coming months.

The boost from the public work programs cannot sustain the job market

Market expectations on the tariff negotiations are quite pessimistic

Overnight, Korea began trade negotiations with the US. It’s heartening that Korea secured a seat at the negotiating table. Yet, expectations for good results are quite low. In fact, it’s quite surprising that Trump agreed to talk to the acting president, Han Duk Soo, at all. Working-level discussions will follow soon. The US wants a package deal on tariffs and military spending. It might include larger direct investments in the US, including a commitment to the Alaska LNG pipeline project and increased imports of energy products like LNG and agricultural goods. Talks also might focus on a significant increase in military spending. Given Trump’s desire for a tenfold rise in Korea’s current spending of US troops in Korea, it’s unclear what concessions Korea will actually make.

Even if the current government makes a deal with the Trump administration, it's not clear that it will be implemented. The opposition Democratic Party (DP) is leading in the polls. If the DP candidate is elected, he may not agree with some of the deals made by the current government. Given that the opposition party controls the parliament, implementation would likely be delayed. The DP is likely to take a stronger stance on military spending and agricultural products. We still believe that negotiations are more feasible after the presidential election on 3 June.

WGBI inclusion delayed to next April

The FTSE confirmed that the Korean Treasury Bond will be included in WGBI index. Inclusion, though, will begin next April, not this October. This reflects delays in system preparation and pursuing for a smoother transition rather than political turmoil in Seoul. However, this news worked against the Korean won (KRW), pushing the currency to the lowest level since 2009.

BoK dilemma between weak KRW and weak growth

Amid rising uncertainty over tariffs, wildfires in the southern provinces and a delay in fiscal support, the Korean economy is likely to grow below 1% in 2025. We have lowered our GDP forecast for 2025 from 1.1% year-on-year to 0.8%. First quarter GDP is likely to grow 0.2% quarter-on-quarter. The current account surplus widened quite sharply in February, thanks to the frontloading of exports. This likely remained the case in March. It should work in favour of first quarter growth despite weak domestic demand. However, the policy vacuum caused by President Yoon’s formal exit last week could slow fiscal support and restructuring efforts in the construction sector. This will weigh on growth from the second quarter. All signs point to more Bank of Korea easing. However, the weak KRW and volatile financial markets are likely to pose major challenges to a prompt BoK policy response.

Expect an April cut, rather than waiting until May

It's a close call, but the BoK is likely to deliver a rate cut in April. Even though the KRW is the lowest in about six years, there’s no guarantee it will stabilise within a month’s time. Our one-month KRW forecast is 1,500. The authorities will try to calm the market, but given global risk-off sentiment, their ability to stabilise things is questionable. Some argue that the weak KRW prompted the BoK to pause its rate cut cycle in January. But we believe the situation is different today than in January. Back then, the weakness was driven by the domestic political turmoil. It has now dissipated somewhat.

Political divisions remain, and so will political uncertainty. But recent KRW weakness is driven more by global events. Thus, the BoK’s response may be different than in January. Also, the global and South Korean growth outlooks are now gloomier than the start of 2025. In addition, we're not sure that the Federal Reserve will give a hint of rate cuts in the near term at its May meeting. ING expects the Fed to cut in July. The Fed's forward guidance could be a catalyst for KRW moves.

A second possible scenario is the BoK staying on hold in April, but with clear hints of a cut in May. This is what we saw in January. Still, this isn’t likely to prevent the KRW from weakening, as the market prices this in beforehand. For these reasons, we now see a slightly higher chance in the April cut than in May.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Commercial Metals Company: Maintaining Bearish Rating As Margin Pressures Continues
data.description:
Intro

We initially became bearish on Commercial Metals Company (NYSE:CMC) in May 2024 and reiterated our stance in November last year. Reasons for our bearishness at the time were sluggish growth, an unattractive valuation, and bearish technicals, all things remaining equal. To try and stabilize CMC's margins somewhat, management had been trying to sell investors on the breath of its newly enacted TAG initiatives (internal efficiency program). Nevertheless, shares are down approximately 29% & 24% since both commentaries. Furthermore, missed EPS estimates in the company's first & second quarters (Q2 earnings announced on 3/20/2025) demonstrate that there may be no end in sight to the current downtrend.

Q2 - Fiscal 2025 Trends

Management reported net earnings of $25.5 million on revenues of $1.75 billion in the quarter. Notwithstanding the earnings miss (GAAP earnings of $0.22), the magnitude of the difference between the comparable quarter in fiscal 2024 (GAAP income of $85.9 million of $0.74 per share) is startling, to say the least. Negative earnings growth trends have consensus expecting $2.89 in earnings this year (Fiscal year ends in August) which if realized would be a 30% contraction versus fiscal 2024.

Then, although forward-looking EPS revision trends continue to come in negative, consensus is projecting earnings of $4.27 in fiscal 2026 (47% year-over-year growth rate). A few thoughts on this expected growth.

Optimistic Fiscal 2026 Growth Expectations

Firstly, investors should remember where the company is coming from. To this point, in fiscal 2022, CMC reported almost $10 a share in earnings but yet the company's prevailing share price ($40.95) looks overvalued compared to what it traded at in fiscal 2022.

Secondly, you feel that management on the recent Q2 earnings call (20/3/2025) was more 'hoping' for better macro conditions than expectant all things remaining equal. The fact of the matter is that although CMC has control over its cost optimization initiatives, the speed at which demand for its products returns at scale remains firmly out of the company's control.

North American Sluggishness Continues

In the company's markets in North America for example, (except data centers, LNG capacity & Highways/Infrastructure), indecision & uncertainty continue to adversely affect the rate at which CMC secures rubber-stamped purchase orders. The pipeline may be growing as a result, but the speed at which this 'pent-up demand (where global tariff regimes & interest rate uncertainty have not been helping) comes to market remains an unknown at this juncture.

In Europe, management pointed to the meaningful increase in projected defense spending and the rebuilding of Ukraine, highlighting the likely 'lift' in construction activity & sales of CMC's steel offerings alike. As alluded to above, however, the speed at which the above is rolled out is crucial to CMC's prospects for the following reasons.

Faster Turning Over Of Capital Required

Ultimately, there are two ways to improve a company's return on capital. One can either grow margins or turn over capital at a faster clip. With the latter being very much dependent on external macro demand-led conditions, management introduced its TAG efficiency program early in fiscal 2025 to try and stabilize margins. To this point, gross margin has fallen from a five-year average of 18.44% to a trailing average of 15.39%. However, as alluded to earlier, it is how earnings have meaningfully collapsed in recent times that is concerning in CMC at this stage.

Net income margin has fallen from a 5-year average of 8% to currently come in at a mere 0.94% over a trailing 12-month average. The problem with the above is that if the gross margin were to continue to deteriorate, it would put significant pressure on the CMC's earnings growth curve over time. Although seasonal factors were in play in the recent quarter, CMC's gross margin came in at a much reduced 12.51%, demonstrating the dire need for this company to get a hold of its costs at the earliest availability.

Earlier this fiscal year, we introduced Transform, Advance, and Grow, or TAG, our enterprise-wide operational and commercial excellence programs, with the goal of generating a permanent improvement in our margin profile. This program is unlike any other ever launched at CMC due to the breadth and depth of its reach as well as its visibility and accountability structures built to support it. Every line of business in every support function, has been involved in identifying and quantifying opportunities that now include over 150 different initiatives. Currently, CMC is executing over 25 first-wave initiatives with very strong early results.

Intermediate Technical Chart

The problem from a technical standpoint is not only does the late 2024 spike above overhead resistance look like a false breakout but now CMC's bearish trend since then seems to be accelerating to the downside. This is confirmed by the rising intermediate ADX indicator which has now surpassed the 20 level on the weekly chart. When this indicator inches above this level, it tends to continue its ascent, illustrating that a trend in motion is more likely to continue here than reverse over time. This in one way demonstrates that now may be a better time to sell CMC as opposed to the dates of our commentaries last year (Trend in Motion).

Conclusion

To sum up, we are reiterating our bearish bias in Commercial Metals Company due to deteriorating margins, and bearish EPS revisions, culminating in an intermediate bearish trend that appears to be gaining traction. Furthermore, even if shares print a temporary bottom over the near term, investors need to consider that shares would have to break out above significant overhead resistance to initiate a fresh bullish trend. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Terrible Twos
data.description:
By Joseph Nelesen

Life has been a little eventful since we last checked in. Under normal circumstances, this blog would review March and Q1 performance of S&P 500® sectors and blends. Yes, tariffs are the elephant in the room we’ve been unable to avoid all year, but as of April, that elephant seems to have invited the rest of the herd inside for a rampage.

In April, the tariff narrative went global, sparking a 10.5% drop in The 500TM over the course of two days, the steepest two-day decline since the dawn of the COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020. As shown in Exhibit 1, no sector was spared from the pain over the first week of April, with performance ranging from a 3.8% drop for Health Care to Energy’s 15.3% fall.

Although every sector declined YTD in April, not all fell as much as the broader market. At any given time, at least one sector must be outperforming the others, with historically cyclical and defensive sectors faring better or worse depending on market direction. In a previous blog, we illustrated the relative performance of cyclical and defensive blends of sectors through the first two months of 2025, using a simple methodology of creating two blends that combine five higher-risk or five lower-risk sectors, as discussed in recent research.1

In Exhibit 2, we show daily excess returns of each five-sector blend (cyclical and defensive) for every trading day of 2025, through April 4.

Two observations might stand out from this plot, one quite intuitive and the other perhaps more curious. First, the upward sloping trend of the cyclical data points and the downward slope of the defensive blend data illustrate that each has had a historical tendency to directionally outperform in markets where we would expect them to. Second, the spread of excess performance outcomes for the cyclical blend appears more tightly grouped than that of the defensive blend. Why might this be so?

The answer is that cyclical sectors are where we find most of the Magnificent 7, which have been responsible for a disproportionate share of The 500’s decline. More precisely, while the Magnificent 7 averaged 31.9% of total weight in the benchmark YTD through April 4, they were responsible for contributing more than half of the benchmark’s decline over the same period. Many of these stocks were already on unsteady ground after the disruption of the AI competitive landscape in Q1, and now that tariffs have been announced to be broader and larger than anticipated, their sectors' higher-than-average foreign revenue exposure has caused a new wave of worries.

Exhibit 3 illustrates the weight of each blend in The 500 and the proportion of Magnificent 7 weight in each, suggesting that the cyclical blend’s fortunes may be more closely (but not entirely) tied to those of the largest stocks in the benchmark, which would make relative outperformance more constrained when those large stocks lead performance up or down.

Despite this concentration of mega-cap stocks in the cyclical blend, it has still managed to slightly outperform The 500 by 0.3% YTD, as shown in Exhibit 4. Of course, the defensive blend has withstood the barrage even better, outpacing the benchmark by 9.9%.

1 Cyclical and defensive blends are composed of the top five and bottom five sectors as ranked by historical beta and volatility. Real Estate, ranked in the middle of the 11 S&P 500 sectors, is excluded from this analysis.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Daimler Truck: The Tariff Tug-Of-War Endangers Its Margins
data.description:
Introduction

Tariffs. We have not received any further updates since April 2. The shock was huge, and many have joked about being "liberated" from excessive gains. Economists, politicians, journalists, investors, and all of us have researched, discussed, and formed an initial understanding and what is going on. In these turmoiled waters, I am reviewing all of the industrial stocks I cover, since most of them will, in one way or another, be hit at least by tariffs on imported steel and aluminum.

Cross-border activity surged in March, as importers rushed to transport more imported goods into the U.S. before tariffs kicked in. Trucking played a big role, and this made me examine how Daimler Truck (OTCPK:DTGHF; OTCPK:DTRUY), the largest truck manufacturer in the U.S., could be affected by the new economic environment.

Luckily, Daimler Truck reported its Q4 and FY24 earnings in mid-March, and during the earnings call, it was possible for its management to address the topic of tariffs.

Summary of previous coverage

First of all, a quick summary of what Daimler Truck does and what the state of the art with this business. In late 2021, it was spun off from Mercedes-Benz in late 2021. It owns and produces trucks and buses under the brands Freightliner, Western Star (vocational trucks), FUSO, Mercedes-Benz, BharatBenz, and Thomas Built Buses.

As the company began its stand-alone journey, it immediately targeted boosting its profitability and achieving industry-leading margins above 10%. Among its strengths, Daimler Truck showcases its footprint in North America, where it is the undisputed leader for heavy-duty trucks, with a market share of around 40%. As Daimler Truck benefited from strong order intake during the post-pandemic recovery, I chose to go long, and I held the stock for around a year. Then, I exited it as Daimler Truck saw a deteriorating outlook due to declining orders.

Daimler Truck's FY2024 Results

In 2024, Daimler Truck reported a 12% decrease in unit sales to 460,409 (FY23: 526,053). Total revenues were down 3% to EUR 54.1B (FY23: EUR 55.9B), with EUR 50.7B coming from the industrial business. As we move down the income statement, Daimler Truck saw its margins under pressure, reporting a 31% decline in EBIT to EUR 3.5B (FY23: EUR 5.2B) and a RoS of 6.9% (FY23: 9.4%). Net profit decreased by almost EUR 1B (-23%) to EUR 3.1B.

As we can see, while North America held up rather well, achieving a 12.9% RoS, Mercedes-Benz Trucks (EUR30 and Latin America), and Trucks Asia performed poorly, with sharp declines in sales and margins.

Unfortunately, order intake was down 2% YoY, even though Daimler Truck reported a strong 31% QoQ increase. The company started FY2025 with a book-to-bill ratio of 91%. Generally speaking, we would always like to see a ratio above 100%, but since this is almost impossible due to the cyclical nature of transportation, we see the company is still in good shape. The geography of order intake is, however, mixed: EMEA is down 15% while Latin America is up 47%. In North America, Daimler Truck keeps seeing a freight recession, but its vocational truck division is still enjoying strong momentum.

Now, in March, Daimler Truck reported that in the last few weeks, order intake has been softer due to the ongoing uncertainty. And here we are back to tariffs, which should now be considered as part of the equation that doesn't make this fiscal year look promising for the company.

Daimler Truck's Manufacturing Footprint

Daimler Truck is headquartered in Portland, OR, where manufacturing for Western Star and Freightliner takes place. It also has plants in North Carolina and in South Carolina. It doesn't have operating plants in Canada, but it has several manufacturing sites in Mexico, where Freightliner and heavy-duty trucks are produced, as well as buses, chassis, and other components (engines, electronics, and transmissions).

To this extent, the newly appointed CEO Karin Radstrom explained during the earnings call how Daimler Truck can handle the new hurdles on imports:

It is reasonable to expect that persisting uncertainty around or implementation of tariffs would hurt our volume and profitability projections. The ongoing uncertainty has already impacted order intake in the last few weeks, which might lead to a softer quarter, too [...]

We have assembly factories in the U.S. as well as in Mexico. That's good in the situation we face right now is that we can produce all models in the U.S. or in Mexico. So, for sure, we are preparing ourselves for different scenarios. And we can ramp up more in the U.S. if that would be required. And we're looking at it both from the kind of a shorter-term perspective, how we could increase capacity in the U.S., and also from a longer-term perspective, what we could do.

The good news is that Daimler Truck can produce its models both in the U.S. and in Mexico. Moreover, most of the parts it assembles are USMCA-compliant. So far, therefore, the impact on Daimler Truck has been caused by two events: lower consumer confidence and tariffs on steel and aluminum.

Valuation

Daimler Truck currently trades at a TTM PE of 9. But the fwd PE is around 16. This is rather high, and it shows that the cycle has peaked and that investors are pricing a decline in earnings. This sets the stock in a difficult situation, especially with the ongoing commercial stress caused by tariffs. It is difficult to estimate the impact of these measures on Daimler Truck's earnings, but with a book-to-bill ratio below 100% and news of softening demand, it is hard to imagine that this year Daimler Truck will be able to improve its profitability. I think a 5% RoS would be a reasonable target, but this clearly makes the stock too risky. We should also not be tricked by the juicy 9.6% dividend yield, which is based on the past dividend. Daimler Truck distributes around 40% to 60% of the group's net profit. But with FY24 earnings already down by 23% YoY, I don't see this fiscal year as the year of a rebound. In fact, this year it will pay in May a dividend of EUR 1.90, which is a 5.7% yield. It is still hefty, but we should also consider the German withholding tax of 26% (the stock is listed in Frankfurt).

Conclusion

Freight transportation is the backbone of the economy. With tariffs shielding the American economy, we may be witnessing a redesign in logistics and a shift towards local-for-local strategies. Daimler Truck is in the midst of this change, but since we are in the very early innings, it is hard to predict how the company will sail through turmoiled waters. Analysts seem cautious about the stock, and although Daimler Truck's presence in North America provides some resilience, the unpredictability of manufacturing costs and softer demand suggest this year will be particularly challenging for the company. As a result, I suggest exiting the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
